publication_title: Systemic Neutrophil Priming and Inflammasome Activation in Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells of Treatment-Naïve Bullous Pemphigoid Patients: A Transcriptomic Analysis Revealing Novel Therapeutic Targets

biological_process_synthesis.text: The gene expression signature reveals a profound activation of neutrophil-mediated innate immune responses in bullous pemphigoid patients' peripheral blood. Marked upregulation of S100 alarmins (S100A8, S100A9, S100A12), antimicrobial peptides (CAMP, LTF, BPI), and neutrophil granule proteins indicates systemic neutrophil priming and degranulation. The inflammasome pathway activation (AIM2, CASP1, IL1R2) coupled with tissue-remodeling enzymes (MMP8) and inflammatory cytokines (OSM) suggests a coordinated inflammatory cascade. Alongside this acute inflammatory program, expression of regulatory molecules (ANXA1, IL1R2, ARG1) indicates attempted counter-regulatory responses. This molecular profile reflects the systemic inflammatory state characteristic of autoimmune blistering diseases, with neutrophils serving as key effector cells beyond their local tissue infiltration.

terms: Neutrophil activation involved in immune response

statements: Circulating neutrophil priming hypothesis: Neutrophils in BP patients are systemically pre-activated in circulation before tissue infiltration, evidenced by peripheral S100 alarmin expression. Interventions targeting neutrophil priming (e.g., CXCR2 antagonists) may prevent blister formation more effectively than local anti-inflammatory approaches.

Inflammasome-driven disease persistence hypothesis: Sustained NLRP3/AIM2 inflammasome activation in PBMCs perpetuates disease chronicity through IL-1β production. IL-1 receptor antagonists (anakinra) may serve as novel steroid-sparing therapeutics in treatment-resistant BP.

Systemic metabolic reprogramming hypothesis: The coordinated expression of metabolic genes (PDK4, ACSL1, ACSL4) suggests neutrophils undergo metabolic shifts toward glycolysis and fatty acid metabolism to sustain inflammatory functions. Targeting metabolic checkpoints could represent a novel therapeutic avenue.

Biomarker-guided stratification hypothesis: The ratio of pro-inflammatory (S100A8/A9) to counter-regulatory (ANXA1, ARG1) molecules in PBMCs may predict disease severity and treatment response, enabling personalized therapeutic approaches.

Microbiome-immune axis hypothesis: Elevated antimicrobial peptides and pattern recognition receptors (TLR5) suggest dysbiotic microbial signals may drive systemic immune activation in BP patients, warranting investigation of the skin and gut microbiome composition.

Systemic inflammasome signature: The prominent expression of multiple inflammasome components (AIM2, CASP1, NLRP3 pathway genes) in circulating PBMCs suggests inflammasome activation is not restricted to lesional skin but represents a systemic phenomenon. This challenges the view of BP as purely a local autoimmune process.

Myeloid suppressor phenotype coexistence: The unexpected presence of immunosuppressive markers (ARG1, IL1R2 as decoy receptor) alongside intense inflammatory signals suggests concurrent activation of counter-regulatory mechanisms, potentially explaining variable treatment responses among patients.

Neutrophil metabolic reprogramming: The coordinate expression of fatty acid metabolism genes (ACSL1, ACSL4, PDK4) alongside classical neutrophil activation markers suggests metabolic rewiring supports sustained inflammatory function, an underappreciated aspect of BP pathogenesis.

Extracellular matrix interaction signature: Genes involved in ECM binding and remodeling (COL4A1, MMP8, MYOF) in circulating cells may indicate pre-programming of neutrophils for tissue infiltration and basement membrane disruption before they reach skin tissue.

systems: Sepsis and systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)

modules.associated_phenotype: Systemic neutrophil priming and antimicrobial response
Extracellular matrix modification and cell migration

terms: Neutrophil activation involved in immune response

summaries.summary: The Gene Ontology analysis reveals bullous pemphigoid as fundamentally a disease of dysregulated innate immunity with prominent neutrophil activation signatures. The most significantly enriched processes center on neutrophil degranulation, antimicrobial humoral responses, and inflammatory cytokine production, indicating that circulating immune cells are systemically activated rather than responding solely at local tissue sites. The presence of counter-regulatory processes (negative regulation of immune response, apoptotic signaling) alongside pro-inflammatory pathways suggests the immune system is attempting but failing to resolve inflammation. Pattern recognition receptor signaling and inflammasome assembly terms highlight the role of danger-associated molecular patterns in perpetuating the inflammatory cycle.

KEGG pathway analysis emphasizes the central role of inflammasome signaling, particularly NLRP3 and AIM2 pathways, in driving IL-1β-mediated inflammation in BP patients. Fc gamma receptor-mediated phagocytosis pathways suggest enhanced antibody-dependent cellular responses, consistent with the autoantibody-mediated pathogenesis of BP. The enrichment of proteasome pathway components indicates active protein turnover and antigen processing, while metabolic pathways (including fatty acid metabolism and glycolysis-related genes) reveal metabolic reprogramming that supports sustained inflammatory cell function. Cell adhesion molecule pathways provide molecular mechanisms for enhanced leukocyte recruitment and tissue infiltration.

Reactome analysis highlights the granule exocytosis machinery and innate immune signaling cascades as core features of the BP transcriptional signature. The prominence of antimicrobial peptide production pathways and S100 protein networks underscores the alarmin-driven nature of inflammation in this disease. Extracellular matrix organization and degradation pathways reveal ongoing tissue remodeling processes that likely contribute to basement membrane disruption characteristic of BP. The identification of oxidative stress response pathways suggests reactive oxygen species production by activated neutrophils contributes to tissue damage, while cell death signaling pathways indicate both inflammatory (pyroptosis) and regulatory (apoptosis) forms of programmed cell death are active in disease pathogenesis.
